checks.comment: No variability classification in Gaia DR3 vari tables. At P = 1352 d and moderate eccentricity (e = 0.532), tidal modulation near periastron may be present.  At G = 12.3 (bright), photometric precision is excellent and any large-amplitude variability would have been detected.

At G = 12.3, the source is relatively bright.  At low Galactic latitude (b = 2.8 deg), source density is elevated compared to the halo, but RUWE = 9.2 is fully consistent with binary photo-centre motion.  The bright apparent magnitude reduces contamination sensitivity.

No prior identification as a compact-object binary.  At G = 12.3 and b = 2.8 deg, the source may appear in photometric surveys (2MASS, AllWISE) but has not been flagged as a BH candidate in the literature.  This would be a novel discovery.

checks.eRASS: Not yet public at this position
checks.comment: No ROSAT or XMM detection.  At b = 2.8 deg, Galactic absorption (N_H) is moderate, which could suppress soft X-ray emission.  However, the absence of X-ray emission is consistent with a quiescent (non-accreting) BH in a wide, detached system with P = 1352 d.  At periastron, a(1-e) ~ 2.1 AU, much larger than the Roche lobe of the K-giant primary.

At l = 310.4 deg, b = 2.8 deg, d = 1488 pc, z = 0.072 kpc above the plane.  With |z| ~ 72 pc, this source is firmly in the Galactic thin disk.  The measured radial velocity (RV = -5.04 +/- 2.08 km/s) is unremarkable for a disk star, consistent with normal Galactic rotation.  Thin disk membership suggests formation through standard stellar evolution of a massive binary progenitor.

summary: All archival veto checks passed.  Thin disk membership at |z| ~ 72 pc.  Measured RV available.  No prior compact-object identification.
